region_of_origin: Northwestern China, Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Da Pan Ji / Big Plate Chicken. 'Chicken stew' (FOODON_03315595) is the closest defensible match. The dish also contains wheat noodles and potatoes which are not captured by this term alone.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for this braised chicken dish with vegetables and noodles
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Da Pan Ji. A0CKP (cooked chicken) is used as the primary anchor. The noodle and potato components are not individually coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Da Pan Ji or Big Plate Chicken as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard communal serving of Big Plate Chicken is approximately 500 g, comprising bone-in chicken pieces, potatoes, bell peppers, and a generous portion of wide flat wheat noodles soaked in the braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken stew with vegetables and noodles, composite estimate; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition Database for mixed chicken dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Da Pan Ji exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of USDA entries for braised/stewed chicken with vegetables (FDC ID 171477 and related entries) and HPB data for mixed chicken rice dishes, adjusted for the noodle and spice components typical of this dish. Input JSON values (enriched_json, confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with cross-validation. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.2 g/100 g based on USDA chicken stew data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles and mixed chicken stew analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Da Pan Ji. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat flat noodles (GI ~52–58) and potato-based mixed dishes (GI ~65–72), weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution of noodles (~55%) and potatoes (~35%) in the dish. The high protein and fat content from chicken and cooking oil moderates the overall glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (62 × 67.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 41.9, rounded to 44.3 accounting for the full serving carbohydrate load. This is a medium-GI, high-GL dish per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g; ~1900 mg per 500 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content from soy sauce and braising liquid; a 500 g serving provides approximately 1900 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI with high glycemic load per serving from wheat noodles and potatoes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in reduced portions and monitor total carbohydrate intake carefully, as the combination of wheat flat noodles and potatoes delivers a high glycemic load (~44 g GL per standard 500 g serving) despite the moderating effect of protein and fat from chicken.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from chicken skin and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption due to the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (~11 g per 500 g serving), both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk; requesting preparation without chicken skin and with reduced oil can lower the saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (potatoes, bell peppers, chilies) may trigger symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While chicken provides beneficial protein and spices such as ginger have anti-inflammatory properties, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should be cautious with this dish due to its potato, bell pepper, and chili content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Big Plate Chicken (大盘鸡, Dàpánjī), known in Singapore colloquially as 'Big pau (chicken)', is a celebrated Xinjiang dish from Northwestern China that reflects the fusion of Central Asian and Han Chinese culinary traditions. The dish is a hearty, deeply spiced stew of bone-in chicken pieces braised together with potatoes, onions, and bell peppers in a rich sauce built from soy sauce, ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, Sichuan peppercorns, and a generous quantity of dried red chilies. It is traditionally served on a large communal plate and accompanied by wide, hand-pulled wheat flat noodles called Latiaozi or Belt Noodles (Kuaimian), which are laid over the stew to absorb the intensely savoury and spicy braising liquid. The flavour profile is bold, umami-rich, moderately spicy, and warming, with a tender chicken texture and soft, sauce-soaked noodles. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 165 kcal, 9.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 13.5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 380 mg. A standard 500 g serving delivers roughly 825 kcal and approximately 1900 mg sodium, making it a high-energy, high-sodium meal. The dish is classified as medium GI (~62) but carries a high glycemic load per serving due to the noodle and potato components. It is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten from the wheat noodles. Clinically, it is of concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients due to its sodium and fat content, and requires carbohydrate monitoring for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Da Pan Ji exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from a composite of approved USDA and HPB sources for braised chicken with vegetables and noodles. The input JSON values (confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle and potato entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central braised/stewed chicken entries and HPB Singapore mixed chicken dish data; no exact Da Pan Ji entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. Saturated fat (2.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.5 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA chicken stew fatty acid profiles and are approximate. 3. cholesterol_mg is set to -1: detailed cholesterol data for this specific composite dish preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore as of 2026. 4. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific braised dish preparation is not reported in the approved sources consulted. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace for home-style braised chicken dishes per USDA data). 6. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate weighted from University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat flat noodles and potato-based dishes; no direct GI measurement for Da Pan Ji has been published. 7. The input portions array contained a duplicate entry (two identical '1 serving (500g)' records); only one 500 g serving is used. 8. The input glycemic_load was listed as 0.0, which is inconsistent with a 13.5 g/100 g carbohydrate content; this has been corrected to 44.3 per 500 g serving. 9. The input health_suitability listed 'diabetes' as suitable=true; this has been revised to moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes given the high per-serving glycemic load. 10. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples / Bread-based dishes' to 'Meat and alternatives / Poultry-based stew with noodles' as the primary caloric and protein contribution is from chicken, consistent with the dish's identity as a main-course meat dish.
